coredata.dc:description: Despite the growing emphasis on eco-friendly nanomaterials as energy harvesters, scientists are actively searching for metal-free photocatalysts to be used in environmental remediation strategies. Developing renewable resource-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the sole photocatalyst to harvest visible light for efficient pollutant degradation is crucial yet challenging, particularly for addressing the escalating issue of water deterioration. Moreover, the photocatalytic decomposition of H2O2 under visible light irradiation remains an arduous task. Based on this, we designed two types of CQDs, C-CQDs (carboxylic-rich) and A-CQDs (amine-rich) with distinct molecular surfaces. Owing to the higher amount of upward band bending induced by amine-rich molecular surface, A-CQDs efficiently harvest the visible light and prevent recombination kinetics resulting in prolonged lifetimes (25 ps), and augmented charge carrier density (35.7 × 1018) of photoexcited charge carriers. A-CQDs enabled rapid visible-light-driven photolysis of H2O2 (k = 0.058 min−1) and produced higher quantity of •OH radicals (0.158 μmol/sec) for the mineralization of petroleum waste, BETX (i.e. Benzene, Ethylbenzene, Toluene and Xylene) (k = 0.017–0.026 min−1) and real textile wastewater (k = 0.026 min−1). To assess comparative toxicities of both remediated and non-remediated real wastewater samples in a time and dose depended manner, Drosophila melanogaster was used as a model organism. The findings unequivocally demonstrate the potential of remediated wastewater for watering urban forestry.

head.ce:title: Molecular surface-dependent light harvesting and photo charge separation in plant-derived carbon quantum dots for visible-light-driven OH radical generation for remediation of aromatic hydrocarbon pollutants and real wastewater

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite the growing emphasis on eco-friendly nanomaterials as energy harvesters, scientists are actively searching for metal-free photocatalysts to be used in environmental remediation strategies. Developing renewable resource-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the sole photocatalyst to harvest visible light for efficient pollutant degradation is crucial yet challenging, particularly for addressing the escalating issue of water deterioration. Moreover, the photocatalytic decomposition of H2O2 under visible light irradiation remains an arduous task. Based on this, we designed two types of CQDs, C-CQDs (carboxylic-rich) and A-CQDs (amine-rich) with distinct molecular surfaces. Owing to the higher amount of upward band bending induced by amine-rich molecular surface, A-CQDs efficiently harvest the visible light and prevent recombination kinetics resulting in prolonged lifetimes (25 ps), and augmented charge carrier density (35.7 × 1018) of photoexcited charge carriers. A-CQDs enabled rapid visible-light-driven photolysis of H2O2 (k = 0.058 min−1) and produced higher quantity of •OH radicals (0.158 μmol/sec) for the mineralization of petroleum waste, BETX (i.e. Benzene, Ethylbenzene, Toluene and Xylene) (k = 0.017–0.026 min−1) and real textile wastewater (k = 0.026 min−1). To assess comparative toxicities of both remediated and non-remediated real wastewater samples in a time and dose depended manner, Drosophila melanogaster was used as a model organism. The findings unequivocally demonstrate the potential of remediated wastewater for watering urban forestry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is an alarming demand for the scientific community to provide inventive contributions to developing cutting-edge environmental remediation methods due to the grave risk posed by anthropogenic activities and industrial development [1]. Among the array of technologies available to tackle environmental remediation, the emergence of Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) stands out as a potent approach owing to their ability to fabricate extremely reactive oxidative species (ROS) in situ [2–4]. These ROS include the •OH, triplet oxygen (O2 2•), superoxide anion (O2 •─), and peroxide ion (O2 ─2). Owing to its short lifetime of 0.1 ns and apposite oxidation potential (E0 = +3.06 V) [5], •OH radicals are an effective contender for the remediation of hazardous chemicals, which may cause partial or complete oxidation of organic substances. Several AOPs based techniques [6,7], including sonolysis, UV + ozone (O3), UV + hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), supercritical water oxidation, pulsed plasma, UV + ozone (O3), wet air oxidation, and radiolysis, etc., have been developed. The most frequently employed method was the use of Fenton's reagent [8] a mixture of Fe(II)/Fe(III) and H2O2 that degrades organic molecules by producing numerous hydroxyl radicals. Fenton's reagent poses key challenges, including the requirement of very low pH levels (<3) and the development of substantial iron sludge as a by-product, thereby giving rise to potential secondary pollution.

To alleviate the issue of secondary pollution arising from iron sludge formation, a heterogeneous variant of Fenton's reagent has been devised, involving the deposition of iron onto solid supports like zeolite, carbon nanotubes, silica, and clay materials [9,10]. Another method of employing H2O2 to oxidize or degrade organic materials is to use it with an appropriate photocatalyst. H2O2 is effectively used with TiO2 photocatalyst to improve the overall efficacy of the protocol for the degradation of the organic substance [11]. Nevertheless, due to the high bandgap energy, TiO2 shows photocatalytic activity in UV light (λ < 400 nm) [12]. The application of •OH radicals generated by AOPs in large-scale industries hinges upon several factors, including the feasibility of scaling techniques for •OH radical creation, the cost associated with energy and reagent usage, and the availability of fast and precise in-situ measurement methods for high concentrations of •OH radicals. The current challenges faced in the industrial utilization of •OH radicals encompass limitations in scaling up •OH radical production to meet demand, the elevated cost of reagents during AOP procedures, and the deficiency of precise in-situ monitoring techniques for •OH radicals.

Fortunately, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are opening new possibilities for materials engineering. CQDs, which are quasi-spherical particles with a size of 10 nm or smaller, present enticing alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots. They exhibit outstanding photo responsiveness, redox capabilities, stability, non-toxic properties, and easy tunability, making them highly desirable [13]. Recently, CQDs have garnered significant attention as a promising candidate for photocatalysis, primarily due to their remarkable down- and up-converted photoluminescence (PL) properties and their exceptional capacity to harness UV and near-infrared (NIR) radiation [14]. Further on, CQDs can also serve as electron mediators, electron reservoirs, and spectral converters in light-induced transformations [15].

Most commonly, CQDs end with carboxylic (COOH) and hydroxyl (OH) functional groups, which result in unsatisfactory photocatalytic performance when exposed to visible light irradiation. The primary challenges are attributed to rapid charge carrier recombination and limited absorption of visible light [16]. To increase the charge separation in CQDs, it is usually utilized to create a hetero junction with other photoactive semiconductors [17]. Nevertheless, the poor interfacial interaction of CQDs in hetero junction effectively hinders the migration of migrate photoinduced charge carrier, which lowers the photocatalytic performance of the system in visible light. Thus, it is important to advance a simple alternative strategy that slows down the recombination process, improves photo-charge separation, and efficiently harvests visible light-the major portion of sunlight with a better photocatalytic performance by specifically regulating the surface functionalities and the compositions of CQDs. There is a scarcity of documentation on how the manipulation of surface functionalities and elements in CQDs affects the band structure and the separation and recombination of photoinduced e-/h+ pairs when exposed to visible light [18].

However, specific studies highlight the importance of functional groups in CQDs, particularly in relation to their applications in emission and sensing. These applications encompass a wide range, containing biomarker sensing and the detection of heavy metal ions [19]. Although the incorporation of diverse functionalities onto the CQDs surface is considered efficient for photocatalysis, the available research on amine-rich CQDs is quite limited in this regard. Owing to versatile applications, the fabrication of CQDs containing oxygen-rich functionalities (–OH and –COOH) has been widely explored [20]. Numerous of these methods have drawbacks, including multistep manufacturing, difficult setup, expensive starting materials and processes, post-separation approach, necessary strong acidic environment, low yield, and prolonged reaction time. Today, a significant amount of dedication has been invested in the creation of CQDs utilizing biomass resources [21]. However, the majority of these techniques necessitate either additional surface passivation, strong acids or bases, or expensive infrastructure. Besides, in some cases, CQDs with nitrogen functionalities were developed by solvothermal treatments or pyrolysis of different chemical-based precursors [22]. Nonetheless, pre-functionalization and/or post-functionalization amine sources are required for the multistep fabrication of CQDs with nitrogen functionalities. The major constraint associated with these methodologies is the requirement for post-separation techniques such as dialysis, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), size-exclusion chromatography (SEC), and the utilize of corrosive and environmentally arguable chemicals. Hence, there is a strong demand for the development of a facile, effective, and cost-efficient synthetic method to yield functionally diverse CQDs using environmentally friendly precursors.

Continuing our endeavours to develop sustainable protocols [23–27], in this study, water-soluble, fluorescent C-CQDs and A-CQDs have been synthesized via the carbonization of fruits of plants by the facile and cost-effective sustainable method. Several spectroscopic, analytical,and experimental techniques have been used to characterizethe compositional, structural, and morphological characteristics which reveal that both CQDs have nearly comparable carbon skeletons, the only difference being the presence of the functional group. The functional groups of C-CQDs and A-CQDs are rich in carboxyl (–COOH) and amine (–NH2), respectively. By replacing carboxyl groups with amine groups, the modulation of optoelectronic energy levels in CQDs has a profound impact on recombination of charge carriers, catalytic turnover kinetics, and the dynamics of charge pair division. Alongside material characterizations, a widespread investigation was accomplished to understand the photocatalytic activity mechanism of C-CQDs and A-CQDs. This study involved qualitative and quantitative analysis to gain insights into the production of •OH from H2O2 under visible light irradiation. Comparatively, A-CQDs demonstrated superiority as a photocatalytic system, exhibiting a slower recombination procedure, improved photoinduced electron-hole pairs separation, enhanced light-harvesting capabilities, superior redox ability, and an extended lifetime. This superior performance was achieved through precise regulation of the band structure, in analogize to C-CQDs, resulting in enhanced production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). A rapid in-situ technique such as UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments was employed for the measurement and detection of •OH. Additionally, the potential application of the proposed CQDs-based photocatalytic system extends beyond the decomposition of H2O2. It encompasses the degradation of carcinogenic aromatic hydrocarbons and the treatment of actual wastewater under photocatalytic conditions. The influence of various operational parameters and the reaction kinetics was also investigated. Additionally, the A-CQDs-H2O2 system exhibited outstanding efficacy in treating wastewater samples, as evidenced by impressive improvements in water quality parameters including dissolved oxygen (DO), total dissolved solids (TDS), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), and chemical oxygen demand (COD). The model organism Drosophila melanogaster was used for assessing the toxicity of the remediated and non-remediated wastewater. The outcomes exhibited a substantial decline in toxicity levels following treatment, displaying a clear correlation with the concentration of the treatment approach.

For the experiment, starting materials and chemicals were utilized without additional purification. The specimens of “Trichosanthes dioica” (Pointed gourd or Parwal) and Grewia asiatica (phalsa) were collected locally during the summer. Ethylbenzene, Salicylic acid, DMPO, NaNO3, Na2SO4, AgCl, NaN3, Na2S2O3, CaCl2, Fe(NH4)(SO4)2 and Xylene, Toluene, Hydrogen peroxide 30 %, Terephthalic acid, Benzoquinone, Ethyl acetate, Methanol, AgNO3, KI, MnSO4, NaOH, H2SO4, MgSO4, KCl, FeCl3·6H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, K2Cr2O7 used in this study were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (>99.5 %) and Thermo Scientific (>99.5 %).

The Supplementary Information provides an elaborate account of characterization, encompassing optical, electrochemical, and photoelectrochemical measurements. It also delineates the methodology employed, furnishes details about the instrumentation used, presents information about water quality parameters, and reports findings from recyclability tests.

In this study, green, fluorescent carbon quantum dots A-CQDs and C-CQDs were produced by carbonizing Trichosanthes dioica (Parwal) and Grewia asiatica (phalsa), respectively. The plant material underwent a drying procedure followed by carbonization in a muffle furnace at a temperature of 300 °C for a duration of 3 h. The resulting product was then finely ground applying a mortar and pestle. The obtained powder was subjected to an ultrasonic dispersion method, where a specific quantity was disappeared in 100 ml of ultrapure water. The resulting mixture, which appeared black, was subjected to centrifugation (12,000 rpm for 15 min) and filtered through a microporous membrane with a pour size of 0.22 μm. The obtained solution of carbon quantum dots exhibited a brownish color under daylight and a greenish hue in the long ultraviolet region. The solution was kept at 4 °C for future procedure, and when required, it was freeze-dried to obtain dried CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic experiment for degradation of BETX and real textile industrial wastewater (WW)
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture comprising of 10 ml of BETX (80 mg/L) or 30 ml of WW (30 ml/L), 5 ml of 30 % H2O2 solution, and 5 ml of 0.9 M CQDs was loaded into a 100 ml double-walled quartz photoreactor equipped with a water chiller. The reaction took place at room temperature with continuous stirring, using a 35 W tungsten lamp as a light source equipped with a UV-CUT filter (light intensity; 0.318 W/cm2). At numerous time gaps, small aliquots of the solution were withdrawn for further analysis. The visible-light-driven decomposition of the organised samples was then assessed and analyzed using gas chromatography (GC) or UV–Vis analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing remediation efficiency using Drosophila melanogaster model
ce:section.ce:para: The study utilized wild type Oregon R + flies obtained from the Cytogenetics Laboratory, Department of Zoology, BHU, Varanasi, India. The flies were raised under standard culture conditions with a temperature of 25 ± 1 °C and 60 % humidity, on a standard agar-cornmeal-sugar-yeast food [28]. The experiment consisted of two stages. In the first stage, a dose–response experiment was conducted to determine appropriate concentrations for evaluating the effectiveness of the remediation process in the second phase. For dose response, six treatment groups were established, including a control group (0 %) and experimental groups with varying WW concentrations: 2 %, 10 %, 20 %, 40 %, and 50 %. Synchronized larvae were obtained using egg chambers set up on food petri plates following the method by Dani et al. [29]. First instar larvae were transferred to each experimental food vial. An eclosion assay was conducted to observe larval activity, pupariation, and adult fly emergence. Based on the data, percentage of eclosed flies (eclosion efficiency) was calculated. The assay was carried out with six replicates, each containing 20 larvae [30]. WW and rWW abbreviations are used for real textile industrial wastewater and remediated industrial textile wastewater, respectively.

The second stage involved assessing the impact of remediated wastewater using selected concentrations from the dose–response study. The second experiment comprised five groups: Control (0 % WW), 20 % WW, 50 % WW, 20 % rWW, and 50 % rWW, with rWW representing remediated wastewater. Each group was tested in six replicates. Eclosion efficiency was examined as above. Negative geotaxis behavior was assessed using the adult climbing assay. Flies that developed on WW/rWW food vials were tested for their vertical climbing capabilities. The number of flies surpassing the 10 cm mark within the first 10 s was recorded [31]. Morphological features and anomalies in larvae, dead pupae, eclosed flies, and pupal cases were observed under a stereomicroscope. The total number of anomalies observed in each group was recorded as the anomaly score. Statistical analysis was conducted utilizing GraphPad Prism software. The analysis utilized RM-ANOVA and Tukey's Multiple Comparison Test, setting the significance level at p < 0.05. Detailed methodology can be found in the supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of bio-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The amine-rich CQDs (A-CQDs) and carboxyl-rich CQDs (C-CQDs) were prepared using fruits of Trichosanthes dioica (amino acids-rich biomass source) [32] and Grewia asiatica (carbohydrates-rich biomass source) [33] renewable natural resource respectively (Fig. S1a-b). Both plants are cultivated mainly as a vegetable and possess a range of therapeutic applications. CQDs were synthesized by the carbonization of fruits of plants via a facile and cost-effective sustainable approach as illustrated in Fig. S1c. As far as our knowledge extends, the synthesis of CQDs using fruits of Trichosanthes dioica and Grewia asiatica via carbonization has never been achieved before. One of the intriguing aspects of this synthesis method is that it doesn't use any acid or basic environment or surface passivating agent to start the carbonization method. The obtained CQDs were characterizedby several analytical techniques to validate their morphological, structural, and composition features.

In the X-Ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of C-CQDs, a prominent peak is observed at around 22.12° (2θ angle), indicating the presence of the (002) reflection, which relates to an interlayer spacing (d) of 3.85 Å. Weaker peaks can also be observed at different 2θ angles, specifically 43.72° and 56.7°, corresponding to the (101) and (004) reflections, respectively (Fig. 1 a-c). In contrast, A-CQDs exhibit their most prominent peak at the 2θ values 21.5° of the (002) plane (d = 4.15 Å), with additional weaker peaks at 42.34° and 55.8°, corresponding to the (101) and (004) planes, respectively [34] (Fig. 1d-e). The (002) peak of A-CQDs is positioned closer to the lower 2θ angle compared to C-CQDs. Both CQDs have larger interlayer spacing than graphite (d = 3.36 Å), can be ascribed to the existence of a disordered carbon network, as well as surface defects and functional groups [35]. Rietveld refinement analysis of the XRD data provided valuable insights into the crystal structure and space group of the CQDs. The refined data for C-CQDs revealed a hexagonal system characterized by the space group P63/mmc, whereas the A-CQDs exhibited an orthorhombic arrangement with the space group Fmmm [36]. Rietveld refinement demonstrated a good fit between the experimental and calculated XRD patterns, supported by the low Bragg R-factor and Rf-factor. The refined crystal structure closely matched the experimental diffraction data, confirming the accuracy of the refinement. The estimated lattice parameters, as shown in Table S1, were in excellent agreement with the standard data [37].

The quasi-spheres of CQDs, with size distribution range from 2 to 8 nm, can be noticed in high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1g,k & Fig. S2). The dots in A-CQDs have an agglomeration and are arranged in clusters. The agglomeration detected in A-CQDs could potentially be ascribed to the high surface energy present, which results in a strong attraction between the nanoparticles [38]. Using Image-J software, the statistical particle size distribution analysis determined the average size of C-CQDs to be 4 ± 0.5 nm and A-CQDs to be 3 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1h, l). The absence of lattice fringes, which is also shown by the SAED pattern, supports the amorphous nature of C-CQDs. However, as shown by the SAED pattern, a substantial portion of a particle in A-CQDs also contains an amorphous carbon structure. Nevertheless, owing to theexistence of partial fringes, it can be concluded that a small amount of carbon may also exist in the partially crystalline form in A-CQDs (Fig. 1i,m). Additionally, the XRD patterns stated above and the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of C-CQDs and A-CQDs both support the (002) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1j,n). This behavior stayed in good agreement with the literature data where CQDs exhibited both amorphous morphology and graphitic crystalline nature determine by SEAD and XRD respectively [39–42].

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis of C-CQDs demonstrates a variation in topographic elevation, spanning from 0.5 to 8 nm, and demonstrating an average height of 4 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. S3j–m). This suggests that most C-CQDs consist of approximately 1–12 layers of graphene. Conversely, the AFM analysis of A-CQDs indicates a broader range of heights, spanning from 1 to 150 nm, with an average height of 70 ± 5 nm (Fig. S3n–q). This indicates that A-CQDs have higher graphene layers than C-CQDs. The root mean square (rms) roughness of A-CQDs is higher than that of C-CQDs with increasing height. The surface roughness of A-CQDs is also seen to be enhanced in comparison to C-CQDs in scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. S3a–d). Since the photoactivity of the materials extremely depends on their morphology and the extent of the photoactive layer, the improvement of the photoactive layer in A-CQDs may enhance their photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the related energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping images reveal the uniform dispersion of components and are comparable to the SEM morphological images of both CQDs (Fig. S3e–i). While C-CQDs are made up of carbon and oxygen elements whereas A-CQDs are composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements (Fig. S4).

The exact composition of the C-CQDs whereas A-CQDs was further analysed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and the results are comparable with the EDX study (Table S2). The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 2
e) clearly demonstrate that the synthesized C-CQDs predominantly consist of carbon and oxygen. The peaks corresponding to carbon and oxygen are seen at binding energies approximately 284.7 and 531.5 eV, respectively. In the C1s spectrum of C-CQDs (Fig. 2f), five distinct peaks are detected. These peaks correspond to specific carbon bonding configurations, with binding energies of 284.6 eV (CC, CC), 285.5 eV (CO, COC), 286.8 eV (CO), 287.9 eV (O–CO), and 291.7 eV (π-π* transition). The O1s spectra revealed two peaks at 530.9 eV (CO) and 532.3 eV (CO, O–CO) [43,44] (Fig. 2g). The XPS spectrum of A-CQDs shows distinct peaks at different energy levels corresponding to different elements and bonds present in the material (Fig. 2a). The N1s peak at around 399.7 eV indicates the presence of nitrogen in the A-CQDs. The N1s spectrum [45] (Fig. 2c) shows three main peaks centered at 398.0 (C-NH2), 399.7(CNC, CN), and 401.4 eV(N-C3). An additional shoulder peak at approximately 406.4 eV is detected, which could be allocated to nitrogen oxides. The C1s peak at approximately 284.6 eV is designated for carbon, and upon high-resolution analysis (C1s peak), it can be further deconvoluted into six Gaussian peaks (Fig. 2b) [43]. The peak centered at 284.5 eV is credited to graphitic CC and CC bonds. While the peaks located at 285.2 and 286.1 eV represent carbon in C–OH, CN, COC and CN bonds, correspondingly. The peak observed at 288.5 eV is associated with CO and N–CO bonds. Additionally, peak around 293.1 eV, likely attributed to the π-π* transition [43], and another peak at 296.2 eV, which may be related to satellite peaks [46]. The O1s peak at around 531.4 eV shows the existence of oxygen in the A-CQDs. Two O1s peaks at 530.9 and 532.4 eV (Fig. 2d), correspond to CO, CO, and N–CO bonds, correspondingly. The comparative analysis presented in Table S3 highlights distinct variations in the relative distributions of atomic and chemical bonds within CQDs.

The outcomes obtained from the XPS analysis are also substantiated by the fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) study (Fig. 2h). Specifically, the FTIR analysis showed that the C-CQDs have a band at 1063 cm−1 which verified the existence of a COC stretching band. Furthermore, the absorption bands observed at 558, 906, 953, 1014, 1703, and 3314 cm−1 correspond to specific vibrational modes. In particular, the vibrational bands detected in the spectrum are allocated as follows: the band at 558 cm−1 resembles to the stretching of Ar-H bonds [47], the band at 906, 953, 1014 due to CC, CO, and CO respectively, the band at 1703 cm−1 resembles to the stretching of CO bonds (–COOH), and the band at 3314 cm−1 resembles to the stretching vibrations of OH bonds. The FTIR analysis of A-CQDs revealed discrete bands related with several vibrational modes. These modes involved the CC, CO, CO and COC stretching vibration detected at 912, 963, 1016 and 1070 cm−1, the CO stretching (–CONH2) detected at 1632 cm−1, the NC=N stretching detected at 2134 cm−1, and the NH in-plane stretching vibrations of amine groups observed at 3408 cm−1. Overall, the FTIR and XPS spectra indicate that both C-CQDs and A-CQDs have hydrophilic surfaces which can enhance their aqueous solubility.

Raman analysis of the synthesized CQDs has been performed to further corroborate the structure associated with the kind of defects and disorder [48]. The Raman spectra of C-CQDs (Fig. S5a) exhibited distinctive graphitic features. Notably, the G band at 1589 cm−1 can be accredited to the presence of sp2 bonds within the graphitic core (resembling graphite-like structure). Additionally, the D mode at 1459 cm−1 is associated with symmetry alterations caused by defects [34]. Likewise, the Raman spectra of A-CQDs also show two definite bands. In the obtained spectrum, the D band is found at 1461 cm−1, whereas the G band is detected at 1592 cm−1 [49]. The ID/IG ratio for A-CQDs is higher (0.78) than that for C-CQDs (0.59). This higher value of the ID/IG ratio in A-CQDs suggests that the nitrogen and amino groups have caused a structural distortion in the carbon structure of the material. These findings align with the results derived from the XPS and FTIR analyses, providing further support and consistency to the overall results. In terms of the zeta potential (ζ), the surface charge on both CQDs is also measured [50] (Fig. S5b). C-CQDs exhibit a negative surface charge. As the pH increases, their zeta potential decreases. Specifically, when the pH is raised to 9, the zeta potential of C-CQDs decreases, indicating their acidic nature. This can be credited to the ionization of carboxyl groups on CQDs surface. In an acidic environment, the ionization of carboxyl groups is weak, resulting in a slightly negative zeta potential for C-CQDs. As the pH increases, the ionization of C-CQDs increases, leading to a more negative zeta potential. Conversely, A-CQDs behave differently because of the existence of amine functionality. The amine groups capture H+ ions in the aqueous solution, leading to their protonation. As a result, A-CQDs exhibit a strong positive charge in a low-pH environment and are neutral in neutral or basic environments. The studies discussed above have collectively validated the morphological, structural, compositional, and functional characteristics of the obtained CQDs, providing comprehensive confirmation of their properties. The obtained results show a comparable profile to those documented for CQDs synthesized using alternative chemical precursors [51].

The optical properties of CQDs play a key role in their practical application in optoelectronics and photonics. To discover the optical-physical characteristics of the CQDs, UV–vis spectroscopy was used (Fig. 3a). The C-CQDs samples exhibit a distinct absorption spectrum with a strong absorption at around 207 nm and a shoulder peak at around 299 nm [52]. More specifically, the absorption peak detected at 207 nm is accredited to the π → π* transition of the CC double bond, while the shoulder peak observed at 299 nm is assigned to the n → π* transition of the CO bond. On the contrary, A-CQDs displayed a wide absorption peak centered at 252 nm, escorted by a spectral tail extending from 350 to 650 nm [53]. The peak detected at 252 nm is accredited to the π → π* electronic transitions, whereas the peak shoulder at 310–320 nm was credited to n → π* transitions implying lone pairs in CO, NC, and amino groups (Fig. S6) [54].

Owing to the presence of nitrogen moieties in A-CQDs, the absorption spectra were shifted in the visible region which demonstrates the enhanced light harvesting property of A-CQDs in the visible spectrum as compared to C-CQDs. The bandgap values of C-CQDs and A-CQDs were revealed to be 3.17 eV and 2.81 eV, individually (Fig. S7). The lower bandgap of A-CQDs can be credited to the existence of nitrogen functionalities on surface of CQDs. Nitrogen atoms have a lone pair of electrons, which results in a positive charge on the nitrogen atom due to electron donation to the carbon structure [55]. The higher electron density in A-CQDs results in a decreased bandgap energy, rendering them more absorbent in the visible region. To estimate the light absorption and emission assets of synthesized CQDs, comprehensive examinations were performed using 2D and 3D PL emission map measurements, as well as photoluminescence excitation (PLE) analysis. The luminescent properties of CQDs are manipulated by two key factors: the presence of π-conjugated domains, defined by the carbon core, and the surface states, which are controlled by the carbon backbone and connecting chemical groups [56]. C-CQDs display a low PL quantum yield of 8.4 % with excitation-dependent emission because of various surface trap states (Fig. 3b & Fig. S8a). On the other hand, A-CQDs, which contain nitrogen, exhibit new surface states that promote radiative recombination pathways, resulting in an enhanced quantum yield of 25.3 %. The maximum emission intensities of both CQDs were analyzed to examine the recombination results accountable for PL emission. The PL analysis of C-CQDs discloses a peak emission intensity detected at 423 nm when excited with a wavelength of 320 nm. Gaussian fitting of the 423 nm PL spectrum (Fig. S9a) reveals that it consists of three subpeaks at 403 nm, 457 nm, and 484 nm, corresponding to nonbonding states with transition energies of 3.07, 2.71 and 2.56 eV to highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO). Given structural analysis confirming substantial oxygen content in C-CQDs, the prominent photoluminescence (PL) observed at 423 nm is likely endorsed to energy transitions involving oxygen states [57] (C-OH, –COOH, CO) to HOMO (Fig. 3i). The PLE spectrum of C-CQDs further confirms that the PL at 423 nm (λex = 320 nm) is mainly driven by excitation energies of 3.85 and 3.54 eV (Fig. 3d).

The PL mapping of A-CQDs (2D and 3D) differs significantly from C-CQDs (Fig. 3c & Fig. S8b). When excited at a wavelength of 400 nm, the A-CQDs exhibit a maximum emission peak at 480 nm. Three subpeaks at 464 nm (2.67 eV), 486 nm (2.55 eV), and 529 nm (2.34 eV), are shown when the 480 nm PL emission spectrum is fitted with a Gaussian function (Fig. S9b). It is assumed that the foremost PL at 480 nm was caused by energy transitions from nitrogen states [58] (C–NH2, –CONH2, CN, NCN) to HOMO owing to the high nitrogen content of A-CQDs (Fig. 3i). The PLE results for A-CQDs indicate a primary excitation energy at 3.10 eV, resulting in a PL emission at 480 nm. Additionally, three other excitation energies of 3.13, 3.38, and 3.71 eV were noted (Fig. 3e). C-CQDs demonstrate higher PL intensity UV photons, while A-CQDs exhibit prominent PL intensity from visible region, confirming their strong visible light harvesting property of A-CQDs. Excited electrons within the π* band have the potential to emanate photons promptly or transition to surface states. In the scenario of relaxation to surface states, these electrons have the capability to emit photons through radiative recombination or participate in non-radiative recombination [59] Consequently, both CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent properties in their PL spectra, attributable to the presence of distributed surface states (Fig. S8).

The presence of oxygen-containing groups in C-CQDs is associated with a strong PL, whereas the coexistence of oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups results in a relatively lower PL intensity. This difference is due to the varying degrees of band bending caused by different functionalities on the CQDs surface (Fig. 3j). The integration of oxygen and nitrogen functionality within A-CQDs directs to the development of a new electric field or expanding band bending near their surface. This electric field effectively separates electron-hole pairs, resulting in lowered recombination rates. Conversely, C-CQDs with only oxygen-containing groups on their surface exhibit low upward band bending, facilitating recombination and resulting in increased emission.

To examine the production and recombination of e-/h+ pairs in C-CQDs and A-CQDs, transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) was conducted (Fig. 3f–g & Fig. S10). The study employed a visible range of probe light (450–800 nm) and an excitation pump light at 360 nm, with ultrafast delay times, to examine the system. The TAS results showed a positive excited state absorption (ESA) for both CQDs due to transitions to higher energy levels. However, there were differences in the absorption spectra between the two types of CQDs. C-CQDs exhibited a broad range of absorption with maxima at around 615 nm, which was attributed to the photogenerated charge carriers. In contrast, A-CQDs displayed a narrower absorption profile in the low-energy range, with peak maxima at 670 nm. At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the lifetime of charge carriers in both types of CQDs was determined by computing the emission at a wavelength of 620 nm. The results revealed a notably prolonged lifetime for e-/h+ pairs in A-CQDs (25 ps) compared to C-CQDs (6 ps), suggesting improved separation of photogenerated charge carriers in A-CQDs (Fig. 3h). Photogenerated e-/h+ pairs with extended lifetimes are normally more favourable for contributing to photo-induced reactions [60].

Further evaluation of the competence of photogenerated e-/h+ pairs separation of CQDs was conducted using chronoamperometry measurements with light ON/OFF at 5-second intervals. (Fig. S11b). A-CQDs have a greater chronoamperometry current (photocurrent) compared to C-CQDs, indicating a more competent separation and an extended lifetime of photo-induced e-/h+ pairs across it. Besides, EIS (electrochemical impedance spectra) data also supports the chronoamperometry measurements. The EIS data shows that the arc for A-CQDs is smaller than the arc for C-CQDs (Fig. S11a). This indicates that the A-CQDs have faster interfacial electron transport and lower recombination rates than for C-CQDs, making A-CQDs them a promising material for use in light-induced applications.

The band structure of the CQDs was characterized through Mott-Schottky measurements [61], as depicted in Fig. S11c. The carrier density, band gap, valence and conduction band potentials, flat band potential of the CQDs were computed using Eq-S6 and the subsequent values are depicted in Table 1 .

Both CQD samples have the appearance of n-type semiconductors. The higher positive flat band potential of A-CQDs than C-CQDs suggests more efficient charge transmission at the photoelectrode/electrolyte interface. The conduction band edge of CQDs with respect to normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) was evaluated using flat band edge potentials. Subsequently, the valence band edge locations vs. NHE were determined using the band gap values and conduction band edge positions. The band edge diagrams for both CQDs are presented versus NHE values in Fig. S11d. In A-CQDs, the conduction band edge (CB) is situated at a higher positive potential, whereas the valence band edge (VB) is positioned at a lower negative potential compared to C-CQDs. The band edge plot indicates that the A-CQDs have a more positive CB and a more negative VB as compared to C-CQDs. This difference in band edge structure can lead to higher carrier density values in A-CQDs, which in turn can result in delayed recombination of charge pairs and increased carrier separation ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs for production of •OH radical form visible-light-driven photolysis of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Following the verification of the morphological, structural, compositional, and optical assets of the synthesized CQDs, our examination commenced with the evaluation of their photocatalytic activity in the photo-induced photolysis of hydrogen peroxide into radicals. Subsequently, a thorough qualitative and quantitative analysis of the generated radicals was conducted. The model experiment was carried out under visible light using 5 ml of CQDs (0.9 M) and 5 ml of H2O2. First, the production of •OH radicals was studied employing a UV–Vis assisted technique employing salicylic acid (SA) as a probe molecule [62]. For the investigation, 0.2 mmol of SA was introduced to the model reaction, and samples were obtained at different time intervals. SA reacts with •OH to yield 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (23-DBA) or 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (25-DBA) (Fig. S12). The highest absorbance of SA, 23-DBA, and 25-DBA is at 303 nm, 310 nm, and 329 nm, respectively [62].

Upon examining Fig. 4
a, it is evident that there is a gradual decrease in the maximum absorbance of SA along with a hypsochromic shift, demonstrating the reaction of salicylic acid and •OH. The displacement of maximum absorbance towards shorter wavelengths is observed when hydroxylated products (23-DBA and 25-DBA) are present, reaching their maximum absorbance. Under visible light irradiation, the highest decline in absorbance of SA occurred in the presence of A-CQDs then C-CQDs and blank experiment. To quantify hydroxyl radicals, it is necessary to deconvolute the UV–Vis spectra of SA subjected to hydroxyl radicals into the contributions from SA, 23-DBA, and 25-DBA (Fig. 4b). According to the stoichiometry of the reaction, the loss of SA concentration is equal to the concentration of hydroxyl radicals.

Nevertheless, the photolysis of H2O2 exhibits pseudo-first-order kinetics, as evidenced by the linear correlations between ln (Ct/C0) and time (Fig. 4c & Fig. S13). In terms of •OH concentration, A-CQDs produced the most effective results. A-CQDs have 3 times higher rate constants than C-CQDs for photolyzing hydrogen peroxide into •OH radicals (Fig. 4d). The impact of A-CQD concentration on the rate was also studied. Fig. 4e unambiguously demonstrates that the 0.9 M A-CQDs solution has the highest activity compared to other trials. The explanation for this is attributed to the increased abundance of active sites and photogenerated reactive species found in larger quantities of A-CQDs. Nevertheless, when utilizing a catalyst concentration of 1.2 M, the photolysis did not show improvement, likely due to the screening effect resulting from an excessive amount of catalyst, hindering efficient light absorption. Consequently, a photocatalyst concentration of 0.9 M was employed in the subsequent assays. In addition, it was determined that 5 ml of H2O2 with A-CQDs was sufficient for this conversion; the excess H2O2 had no impact on the procedure suggesting a saturation of •OH radicals (Fig. 4f).

The model conversion was further examined by utilizing Terephthalic acid (TA) as an alternative survey molecule [63] for the observation of •OH via photoluminescence (Fig. 4g). The hydroxylation of TA results in the formation of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH), which demonstrates an emission peak at 426 nm when excited at 326 nm (Fig. S14). After removing the CQDs from the reaction mixture, the PL spectra of the reaction samples were examined. These experimental findings strongly indicate that A-CQDs generate a higher quantity of •OH radicals compared to C-CQDs. The concentrations of •OH radicals were quantified by correlating PL emission intensity of TAOH, and obtained values agreed with the outcomes gained from UV–Vis (Table S4).

The catalytic ability of CQDs in the photodecomposition of H2O2 was experimented utilizing cyclic voltammetry measurements, and outcomes are exhibited in Fig. 4h. It is worth noting that the bare fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode does not show any electrochemical response in the presence of H2O2, whereas the CQDs/FTO electrode demonstrates noticeable reductive and oxidative current responses during H2O2 decomposition under light irradiation. However, A-CQDs exhibit a higher current response compared to C-CQDs. Moreover, the significant disparity in the reductive current further confirms that A-CQDs possess a greater photocatalytic activity for H2O2 decomposition than C-CQDs.

Fig. 4i presents the EPR spectra of various photocatalytic methods used to photolyze H2O2, employing A-CQDs and C-CQDs under illumination using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-1-oxide (DMPO). Both processes displayed a distinct EPR signal corresponding to the DMPO/•OH adduct [64], with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1. Notably, intensities of the DMPO/•OH signal were observed to be higher for A-CQDs compared to C-CQDs, indicating the efficient formation of •OH by A-CQDs. None of the ESR trials yielded a peak corresponding to the DMPO/O2 •- adduct, thus eliminating the involvement of superoxide radicals (O2 •-) in the process (Fig. S15). Combining these experimental findings with the Mott-Schottky (MS) plots of the CQDs, the plausible mechanistic pathway for H2O2 photolysis is depicted in Fig. 4j.

C-CQD possesses an Eg of 3.17 eV, indicating its limited ability to be photoexcited by visible light due to its poor visible light harvesting capability (>400 nm). In contrast, A-CQD exhibits Eg values of 2.81eV, enabling it to easily enter the photoexcitation state through visible light excitation. Specifically, in the case of A-CQDs, CB energy is more negative compared to the potential of hydroxyls radicals, ─OH/H2O2 (-0.73 V vs NHE) [65]. This accelerates the effective reduction of H2O2 by electrons in the CB, leading to the production of •OH and hydroxide ions (─OH). Furthermore, these ─OH are effectively oxidized to •OH by the accumulated holes in the VB, given its more positive VB potential compared to •OH/–OH (+1.9 V vs NHE) [66]. Due to A-CQDs' efficient visible light harvesting property and higher density of photo-generated charge carriers, a greater amount of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) is produced compared to C-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic assay of CQDs/H2O2 system for BTEX degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The subsequent object of the present work aims to develop a practical use of CQDs/H2O2 system to address visible light-driven environmental remediation of hazardous and toxic pollutants. Our selection of BTEX compounds for this study is based on their well-documented environmental and health risks [67]. These carcinogenic volatile aromatic hydrocarbons are more harmful compared to their aliphatic counterparts, primarily due to their higher recalcitrance [68]. BTEX compounds, which are commonly found in surface and groundwater, often originate from petroleum storage tank leaks, spills at pipelines, assembly wells, refineries, storage and distribution terminals [69].

Initially, preliminary control trials were conducted using 10 ml of benzene (80 mg/L) as a representative pollutant, together with 5 ml of A-CQDs (0.9 M) and 5 ml of H2O2, while exposed to visible light. The degradation efficiency was assessed through gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis, and the results were recorded at various time intervals. The GC analysis indicated a distinct peak for benzene at a retention time of 2.1 min. As the irradiation time increased, the GC intensity consistently decreased. Eventually, after 180 min, the GC intensity diminished significantly, indicating the nearly complete decomposition of benzene. However, it is worth stating that visible-light driven degradation of benzene demonstrated a pseudo-first-order kinetic behaviour, as supported by the linear correlation detected in the plot of natural logarithm of the concentration ratio (ln Ct/C0) against time (t) (Fig. 5a–h & Fig. S16).

A-CQDs exhibited remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performance compared to C-CQDs and the used control trial. The rate constants (k) for A-CQDs and C-CQDs were determined to be 0.017 min−1 and 0.002 min−1, correspondingly (Fig. 5a & e). Substantially, the rate constants of A-CQDs were detected to be eight times higher compared to those of C-CQDs. To explore the impact of A-CQD concentration, experiments were conducted with varying concentrations. The optimal loading for the highest degradation efficiency was determined to be 0.9 M of A-CQD with 5 ml of H2O2 (Fig. 5b,f &c,g). Furthermore, the impact of the primary concentration of benzene on the effectiveness of photocatalytic degradation was checked within the scale of 20–100 mg/L. The findings revealed that the highest rate of benzene degradation was attained with an 80 mg/L solution (Fig. 5d,h). Owing to inadequate •OH accessibility and competitive reaction with intermediates, a decrease in rate was observed at higher benzene concentrations. Moreover, several scavengers were used in additional experiments [70], including MeOH (as a scavenger for hydroxyl radicals), silver nitrate (for electrons), KI (for holes), and benzoquinone (for superoxide radicals). The results indicated a decrease in the degradation rate in all cases, except when benzoquinone was consumed as the scavenger (Fig. 5i).

Based on the aforementioned analysis and findings, it is clear that e-, h+, and •OH are the primary radicals responsible for benzene degradation during the photodegradation process (Fig. 5j). The earlier discussion also revealed that A-CQDs effectively generate •OH from H2O2, facilitating the efficient degradation of benzene through hydroxylation, bond cleavage, ring-opening, and mineralization processes [71]. To determine whether this enhanced photocatalytic activity for degrading volatile aromatic hydrocarbons is applicable in general, we further extended our examination to assess the photocatalytic activity of A-CQDs in the photo-induced photodegradation of various other aromatic hydrocarbons, for example toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene. The data presented in Fig. 5k–m & Fig. S17 clearly demonstrate a similar and significant enhancement in activity, resembling the selective degradation observed for benzene. In the absence of H2O2, A-CQDs shows very less removal% of aromatic pollutants (removal % of BTEX is 7–10 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic assay of CQDs/H2O2 system for real wastewater remediation
ce:section.ce:para: Expanding upon these encouraging findings, the CQDs' photocatalytic performance was additionally assessed by examining their ability to degrade industrial dyes by using real textile industrial wastewater (WW). The contaminated wastewaters were stored from Bagru textile area of Jaipur, Rajasthan, India. Bagru is renowned worldwide for its expertise in Indigo dyes and block Printing [72,73]. The colored effluents originating from various cluster units, including dyeing and printing facilities in Bagru, have appealed substantial consideration because of their dual toxicity. These units and industries discharged their effluents openly, causing detrimental effects on the surrounding flora, fauna, and the overall health of the local residents and communities. The adverse impact extended to nearby villages and resulted in increased levels of Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) and other harmful substances, leading to severe damage to human systems and ecosystems. It was found that the water quality in the Jaipur region of Rajasthan was unsuitable for agricultural and industrial purposes due to the high concentration of electrical conductivity. The collected samples were placed in the refrigerator for subsequent processing in the wastewater treatment procedure. The essential characteristics of the WW were analyzed, and the findings are presented in Table S5. The WW demonstrated the highest UV–Vis absorption at 668 nm.

The investigation focused on the degradation efficiency of WW using a 10 ml sample of WW (30 ml/L) with 5 ml of A-CQDs (0.9 M) and 5 ml of H2O2, with visible light irradiation. The efficacy of degradation of aromatic hydrocarbons was measured at numerous time intervals utilizing a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the wastewater (WW) samples, under the catalytic influence of A-CQDs, are illustrated in Fig. 6 a and b for different time intervals. The absorption band gradually decreased in intensity as the irradiation time increased. After 240 min, the absorption band completely disappeared, indicating comprehensive decomposition of WW.

To explore the kinetic behaviour of the wastewater (WW) photodegradation, the experimental data was subjected to analysis using zero-order, first-order, and second-order kinetic models. Fig. 6c and Fig. S18 illustrate the fitting calculations. The first-order kinetic model showed a higher correlation coefficient, implying outstanding agreement between the experimental data and the equation. Fig. 6d, & h illustrates that A-CQDs (0.026 min−1) exhibited the highest WW degradation compared to C-CQDs (0.007 min−1). Notably, the degradation rate of A-CQDs was approximately four times faster than that of C-CQDs, suggesting their potential as photocatalytic reaction candidates. The most notable result was observed at a concentration of 0.9 M A-CQDs with 5 ml of H2O2 (Fig. 6e,i & f,j). A-CQDs exhibit minimal wastewater removal efficiency in the absence of H2O2, with a removal percentage of less than 5 % for water. The study investigated the influence of initial WW concentration, measured in volume percentage, on the optimal dosage of A-CQDs. To accomplish this, different concentrations of WW (10, 20, 30, and 40 ml/L) were prepared. Fig. 6g,k illustrates that the A-CQDs/H2O2 system effectively operates on the sample with a concentration of 30 ml/L of WW. Increasing the WW concentrations further results in a decrease in the photodegradation rate, which can be attributed to two main factors. Firstly, at higher WW concentrations, the surface of the photocatalyst becomes caked with dye molecules present in WW, diminishing its photo efficiency and reducing the formation of reactive species necessary for the reaction. Secondly, higher concentrations of WW lead to an raise in the number of intermediates, stemming in competition between dye degradation and several side reactions. The reduction in degradation percentage observed when •OH scavengers are present provides support for the significant role of •OH in the degradation mechanism of WW when using A-CQDs (Fig. 6l). Fig. 6m demonstrates the visible-light-driven degradation mechanism of wastewater (WW) in the presence of CQDs. A-CQDs' efficient use of visible light, higher charge carrier density, and suitable band alignment enable effective •OH generation from H2O2 photolysis. This •OH radical initiates the chromophore cleavage, CN bond cleavage, ring-opening, and mineralization processes [74] Initially, •OH radical react with the polymeric substance present in WW and degrade them into the low weight aromatic/cyclic product which can further oxidised by •OH radical into short chain organic compounds. Eventually, •OH predominantly mineralizes these short-chain compounds into CO2 and H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recyclability of the photocatalyst and water quality metrics
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic tests were conducted to assess the reusability of A-CQDs and examine their ability to degrade BETX and WW through photodegradation. The results, depicted in Fig. S19, demonstrate the exceptional reusability of the integrated system, even after six consecutive cycles, implying the remarkable stability of the CQDs. Significantly, carbon-based AOP systems generally encounter stability issues stemming from the adsorptive saturation of active sites by oxidized byproducts and lattice corrosion resulting from the occurrence of highly oxidizing ROS [75]. Nevertheless, significant water solubility of A-CQDs efficiently hinders the collection of aromatic intermediates on the surface, thereby dismissing loss or coverage of active sites. This useful characteristic can be ascribed to the hydrophilic nature of the A-CQDs' surface. To validate this, XRD, HRTEM, and XPS analyses were performed on reused A-CQDs, and the results (Fig. S20) indicate the preservation of the CQDs' morphology and structure even after continual utilize.

Fig. S21 revealed the wastewater quality parameters [76], displaying the highest levels of TDS, BOD, and COD in the water containing BETX and raw WW, while DO levels were at a minimum. In contrast, the treated wastewater after A-CQDs treatment, demonstrating a decreased range of DO, TDS, BOD, and COD, along with improved DO values. These substantial changes in the metrics of wastewater quality serve as additional evidence for the mineralization and degradation of BETX and WW.

In this study, we compared the efficacy of the present work to that of previously reported methods (Table S6-7). In contrast to the findings of the previous study, this investigation indicates that A-CQDs exhibit superior efficacy in the photocatalytic degradation of benzene and real wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of comparative toxicity of remediated and non-remediated real wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical usefulness and toxicity of A-CQDs treated real wastewater, we performed toxicity tests on Drosophila melanogaster (fruit flies), an alternative animal model [77] (Table 8). The impact of real wastewater (both A-CQD-remediated (rWW) and non-remediated (WW)) on eclosion efficiency, adult climbing capacity, and morphology in Drosophila melanogaster was evaluated across varying wastewater concentrations. It was observed that eclosion efficiency was significantly reduced in flies exposed to concentrations above 20 % WW concentration (Fig. S22a). However, the use of A-CQD -remediated wastewater led to an improvement in eclosion efficiency for both 20 % and 50 % rWW groups, with 20 % rWW group showing eclosion efficiency comparable to the control, while the 50 % rWW group remained significantly lower (Fig. S22b). Negative geotaxis behavior was assessed using a climbing assay, revealing a significant improvement in climbing efficiency for flies treated with A-CQD-remediated wastewater compared to the 20 % WW and 50 % WW groups (Fig. S22c and Table S9). Furthermore, the total anomaly score analysis showed a reduction in abnormal features across all life stages (larva, pupa, adult, pupal case) in the A-CQD -remediated wastewater-treated groups, with the highest score observed in the 50 % WW group (Fig. S22d and Fig. S23). Major anomalous features observed were blue coloration of larval stages, incomplete eclosion, pupal death, shrunken abdomen, eye deformity, blue coloration among others. Complete evidence on the abnormal features can be found in the supplementary data (Table S10).

The findings of the study reveal that A-CQD can substantially reduce the toxicity of dye-contaminated wastewater, suggesting their potential as an effective method for treating such effluents. The toxic effects of wastewater might be caused due to various mechanisms, including oxidative stress. In a recent investigation conducted by Rahimi et al. [78], the toxic impacts of two disperse textile dyes, Disperse Blue-124 and Disperse Black-9, on key systemic enzymes in the larvae of wild-type Drosophila melanogaster (Oregon R+) were examined. The researchers exposed Drosophila larvae to corn-sugar-yeast nutrition including the dyes at concentrations of 1, 10, and 100 mg/mL for 48 h. In their study, they detected a dose-dependent advancement in the enzymatic activity of acetylcholine esterase, along with alterations in the actions of antioxidant enzymes for example Catalase (CAT, augmented by over 2.5-fold) and Superoxide dismutase (SOD, improved by over two-fold). Moreover, they examined a substantial three-fold increase in Xanthine oxidase activity and levels of lipid peroxidation (LPO), with a dose-dependent relationship. These findings suggest that the effective degradation of WW could contribute to the mitigation of toxicity.

The utilization of Drosophila melanogaster as an experimental model organism for toxicity evaluation in this examine signifies a novel methodology for evaluating the efficacy of wastewater treatment methods. Future research should further investigate the mechanisms by which A-CQD remediate dye-contaminated wastewater and explore the potential of A-CQD for treating other types of pollutants in various contexts. A-CQD -remediated wastewater treatment could be applied to treat effluents from textile and dye industries, reducing the environmental impact and potential harm to aquatic life and ecosystems. Treated wastewater could potentially be used for watering urban forestry. A-CQD -remediated wastewater treatment could be explored as an additional step in municipal wastewater treatment plants to further reduce the presence of dyes and other pollutants before releasing the treated water into natural water bodies.

The current study focuses on the preparation of CQDs (C-CQDs and A-CQDs) with distinct molecular surfaces using a simple and cost-effective sustainable method derived from biomass. Notably, this synthesis approach does not need additional surface passivation or the use of alkaline or acidic environments like the previous ones [34,49]. Various spectroscopic and analytical methods were utilized for the analysis of C-CQDs and A-CQDs, uncovering that the former predominantly concluded with carboxylic functional groups, whereas the latter displayed amine functional groups. The existence of nitrogen functional groups within A-CQDs was noted to broaden the absorption spectrum and promote improved separation of photogenerated charge carriers. This observation was verified through multiple techniques, including optical absorption, photoluminescence, transient absorption spectroscopy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and chronoamperometry measurements. The longer lifetimes (25 ps) and of charge carrier density (35.7 × 1018) of photoexcited charge carriers in A-CQDs (k = 0.058 min−1) enabled rapid visible-light-driven photolysis of H2O2 into •OH radicals, surpassing the performance of C-CQDs (k = 0.018 min−1). Furthermore, the UV–Vis probe, photoluminescence probe, cyclic voltammetry, and spin-trapping experiments confirmed the higher production of •OH radicals (0.158 μmol/sec) with A-CQDs in the optimized conditions. Notably, the A-CQDs/H2O2 system efficiently degraded various carcinogenic aromatic hydrocarbons (BETX) (k = 0.017–0.026 min−1) as well as real textile industry wastewater (k = 0.026 min−1). The degradation procedure through photocatalysis adhered to pseudo-first-order kinetics. The thermodynamic probability of •OH radical production, in accordance with the redox potentials of CQDs, was also discussed. The analysis of DO, TDS, COD, and BOD in the treated samples provides evidence of the elevated mineralization capacity of A-CQDs. In this study, we compared the efficacy of the present work to that of previously reported methods (Table S6-7). In contrast to the findings of the previous study, this investigation indicates that A-CQDs exhibit superior efficacy in the photocatalytic degradation of benzene and real wastewater. Furthermore, the toxicity analysis of A-CQDs treated real wastewater using Drosophila melanogaster model showed that the remediated sample was benign for the environment, agriculture and human health. These findings will spur the development of visible light-sensitive CQDs, making significant advancements in research concerning current environmental pollution, energy challenges, and related areas.
